cards.text: Static member functions have {{c1::no this pointer}} and can only access {{c2::static member variables}} and other {{c2::static member functions}}.
To define a static member function outside the class, use {{c1::ClassName::functionName()}} but do NOT include the {{c2::static}} keyword in the definition.
Static member functions can be called using either {{c1::ClassName::functionName()}} or {{c2::object.functionName()}}, but {{c1::class name}} syntax is preferred.
Static member functions are best used for functionality that is {{c1::related to the class}} but {{c2::doesn't depend on a specific object}}.

cards.front: Why can't static member functions access non-static member variables?
cards.back: Because static member functions have no this pointer - they aren't associated with any specific object, so they don't know which object's members to access.

cards.front: Can a static member function be const-qualified?
cards.back: No. The const qualifier applies to the this pointer, but static member functions have no this pointer, so const doesn't make sense.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Static member functions cannot access non-static member variables like m_value.
cards.explanation: Static functions have no this pointer, so they can't access non-static members which belong to specific objects.

cards.question: How do you define this static member function outside the class?
cards.answer: int Math::add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
cards.explanation: Use ClassName:: but do NOT repeat the static keyword in the definition.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: Two Counter objects are created, incrementing s_count twice. The static function getCount() returns the shared s_count value of 2.

cards.concept: When to use static member functions
cards.explanation:
Static member functions are useful for:
  • Accessing or modifying static member variables
  • Factory functions that create objects
  • Utility functions related to the class
  • Functions that don't need object-specific data
  • Implementing singleton getInstance() methods
examples: int id = IDGenerator::getNextID();  // No object needed

cards.back: A member function that has no this pointer and can only access static members; can be called without an object
